# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains incident evidence about outage duration, affected services, deployment and CI failure chain, detection timeline, notification failure, root-cause identification, and remediation actions.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-07-033
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: incident
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents" (2026)
source_url: https://blog.firetiger.com/postmortem-on-the-march-1-2026-ingest-incident/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Firetiger's ingest system for most customers was degraded for about 8 hours on March 1, 2026 and failed to accept OpenTelemetry data and GitHub webhooks.

## Source Excerpt

"For approximately 8 hours on Sunday, March 1, 2026, Firetiger's ingest system for most customers was in a degraded state, failing to accept OpenTelemetry data and GitHub webhooks."

## Note

The source states duration and affected ingest paths, but does not quantify customer count or event loss.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-07-033
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: method
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents" (2026)
source_url: https://blog.firetiger.com/postmortem-on-the-march-1-2026-ingest-incident/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
A CI race condition with overly aggressive concurrency control caused a build job to be erroneously canceled during PR merge on February 27, 2026.

## Source Excerpt

"A race condition in our CI systems, with excessively aggressive concurrency control, resulted in a build job getting erroneously canceled during merge of a PR on February 27, 2026."

## Note

This evidence identifies an upstream initiating failure in the deployment chain.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-07-033
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: method
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents" (2026)
source_url: https://blog.firetiger.com/postmortem-on-the-march-1-2026-ingest-incident/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
A second CI attribution error made a later deploy job treat the canceled build as complete and update AWS Lambda and ECS service definitions to a non-existent container ID.

## Source Excerpt

"A later deploy job falsely believed the build had completed because of a second issue in our CI system's attribution of build artifacts, and proceeded to roll out the change by updating AWS Lambda and ECS service definitions to point to a non-existent container ID."

## Note

This evidence describes the second failure needed for the invalid rollout to proceed.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-07-033
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: incident
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents" (2026)
source_url: https://blog.firetiger.com/postmortem-on-the-march-1-2026-ingest-incident/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Terraform rejected the Lambda change as invalid and aborted the deployment, but ECS service definitions had already been mutated.

## Source Excerpt

"This rollout was done with Terraform, which rejected the change to Lambda as invalid, aborting the deployment, but it had already mutated the ECS service definitions."

## Note

The deployment both failed and partially changed infrastructure, leaving the system in an inconsistent state rather than a clean rollback.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-07-033
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: claim-support
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents" (2026)
source_url: https://blog.firetiger.com/postmortem-on-the-march-1-2026-ingest-incident/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
After ECS definitions became invalid, new tasks could not be put into production and any exited task could not be restarted.

## Source Excerpt

"ECS's definitions were thus unable to put new tasks into production, and so traffic was not shifted, but the system was in a delicate state: if any ECS tasks exited, they would not be able to be restarted because their definition was invalid."

## Note

This evidence states the operational condition that turned the bad deployment into a latent failure.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-07-033
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: incident
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents" (2026)
source_url: https://blog.firetiger.com/postmortem-on-the-march-1-2026-ingest-incident/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Around 5:48 UTC on March 1, restarted ECS tasks failed to come up and frontend ingest load balancers began returning 503s for all ingest events.

## Source Excerpt

"About 25 hours later, around 5:48 UTC on March 1, the ECS tasks gradually did begin to get restarted, but new tasks couldn't come up, so our frontend ingest load balancers began to respond with 503s to all ingest events, including webhooks and OpenTelemetry data."

## Note

The source ties the visible outage onset to task restarts roughly 25 hours after the faulty rollout state was created.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-07-033
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: metric
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents" (2026)
source_url: https://blog.firetiger.com/postmortem-on-the-march-1-2026-ingest-incident/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Firetiger's separate self-monitoring deployment detected the issue at 6:00, 12 minutes after the problem materialized.

## Source Excerpt

"We use Firetiger (in a separate deployment) to monitor itself, so the system detected this issue at 6:00, 12 minutes after the problem materialized."

## Note

The source gives both the detection time and the 12-minute delay from incident materialization.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-07-033
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: claim-support
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents" (2026)
source_url: https://blog.firetiger.com/postmortem-on-the-march-1-2026-ingest-incident/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
A detection agent flagged the abnormality but initially believed the problem was isolated to GitHub webhook ingest and only attempted to notify human operators.

## Source Excerpt

"This agent detected and flagged the problem, believing it to be isolated to GitHub webhook ingest, and attempted (more on that) to notify human operators."

## Note

The source describes partial problem recognition and attempted escalation, which matters because the later narrative shows notification did not reach normal incident channels.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-07-033
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: result
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents" (2026)
source_url: https://blog.firetiger.com/postmortem-on-the-march-1-2026-ingest-incident/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
At 7:47, Firetiger's issue triage system coalesced multiple ingestion detections and traffic-drop findings into one issue and identified the bad ECR version as the root cause.

## Source Excerpt

"At 7:47, the problem got worse, as more ingest tasks restarted. Our issue triaging subsystem... identified that multiple detection agents had found problems related to ingestion... and also identified that many other agents had found a major drop in traffic. These all got coalesced into one issue, which was easily able to dig in and find the root cause, identifying the bad version in ECR."

## Note

This evidence ties worsening symptoms to successful automated root-cause identification.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-07-033
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: incident
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents" (2026)
source_url: https://blog.firetiger.com/postmortem-on-the-march-1-2026-ingest-incident/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Human operators were notified about 8 hours late because an internal notification-system misconfiguration hid alerts from normal incident management channels.

## Source Excerpt

"Despite the good performance of our agents in finding the issue, it still took us about 8 hours to be notified of the problem. This was due to a second issue in our system."
"Inadvertently, they left this in a state that hid notifications from our normal incident management channels, which resulted in the long period in which we didn't detect the issue."

## Note

The source presents a tension between fast system detection and delayed human awareness due to notification routing failure.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-07-033
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: method
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents" (2026)
source_url: https://blog.firetiger.com/postmortem-on-the-march-1-2026-ingest-incident/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The notification misconfiguration was introduced late Friday while an engineer repeatedly deleted and recreated a policy document during testing of a deployment-attribution feature.

## Source Excerpt

"Late on Friday night, one of our engineers was working on our internal Firetiger deployment's issue notification system."
"While working on testing a new feature for users... they repeatedly had to delete and remake a policy document used to control where notifications could get sent."

## Note

The source identifies the operational context in which the notification-channel failure was created.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-07-033
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: method
citation: Nelson, "Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents" (2026)
source_url: https://blog.firetiger.com/postmortem-on-the-march-1-2026-ingest-incident/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
After the issue report surfaced, Firetiger used Claude Code with MCP, AWS access, and GitHub CLI access to locate the broken deployment, rebuild the image, rerun deploy scripts, and restore service.

## Source Excerpt

"Once the issue was found and we were alerted to it, we were able to quickly resolve it by pointing Claude Code at the Firetiger issue report."
"Using an MCP connection, as well as AWS and Github CLI access, we were able to find the broken deployment. We rebuilt the image, re-ran deploy scripts, and returned to a healthy state."

## Note

The source describes the remediation workflow after root cause identification and alerting.
---

Around 5:48 UTC on March 1, restarted ECS tasks failed to come up and frontend ingest load balancers began returning 503s for all ingest events.

## Source Excerpt

"About 25 hours later, around 5:48 UTC on March 1, the ECS tasks gradually did begin to get restarted, but new tasks couldn't come up, so our frontend ingest load balancers began to respond with 503s to all ingest events, including webhooks and OpenTelemetry data."

## Note

The source ties the visible outage onset to task restarts roughly 25 hours after the faulty rollout state was created.
```

A CI race condition with overly aggressive concurrency control caused a build job to be erroneously canceled during PR merge on February 27, 2026.

## Source Excerpt

"A race condition in our CI systems, with excessively aggressive concurrency control, resulted in a build job getting erroneously canceled during merge of a PR on February 27, 2026."

## Note

This evidence identifies an upstream initiating failure in the deployment chain.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---

contradiction_relevance: none
---
A second CI attribution error made a later deploy job treat the canceled build as complete and update AWS Lambda and ECS service definitions to a non-existent container ID.

## Source Excerpt

"A later deploy job falsely believed the build had completed because of a second issue in our CI system's attribution of build artifacts, and proceeded to roll out the change by updating AWS Lambda and ECS service definitions to point to a non-existent container ID."

## Note

This evidence describes the second failure needed for the invalid rollout to proceed.
```

```markdown
---

---
Terraform rejected the Lambda change as invalid and aborted the deployment, but ECS service definitions had already been mutated.

## Source Excerpt

"This rollout was done with Terraform, which rejected the change to Lambda as invalid, aborting the deployment, but it had already mutated the ECS service definitions."

## Note

The deployment both failed and partially changed infrastructure, leaving the system in an inconsistent state rather than a clean rollback.
```

After ECS definitions became invalid, new tasks could not be put into production and any exited task could not be restarted.

## Source Excerpt

"ECS's definitions were thus unable to put new tasks into production, and so traffic was not shifted, but the system was in a delicate state: if any ECS tasks exited, they would not be able to be restarted because their definition was invalid."

## Note

This evidence states the operational condition that turned the bad deployment into a latent failure.
```
